perturbation.original_text: All actions, cases, suits and proceedings in connection with this Agreement shall be brought in Maryland. All persons affected by this Agreement specifically consent to the personal jurisdiction of and venue in said courts. No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than one (1) year after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than two (2) years from the date of the last payment. ALL ACTIONS, CASES, SUITS AND PROCEEDINGS SHALL BE HEARD WITHOUT A JURY. ALL PERSONS AFFECTED BY THIS AGREEMENT SPECIFICALLY WAIVE ALL RIGHT TO A TRIAL BY JURY AND SPECIFICALLY CONSENT TO THE PERSONAL JURISDICTION OF AND VENUE IN SAID COURTS.
perturbation.changed_text: All actions, cases, suits and proceedings in connection with this Agreement shall be brought in Maryland. All persons affected by this Agreement specifically consent to the personal jurisdiction of and venue in said courts. No action, case, suit or proceeding, regardless of form, arising out of or related to this Agreement, may be brought by either party more than one (1) year after the cause of action has arisen, or in the case of nonpayment, more than two (2) years from the date of the last payment.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the waiver of jury trial, the contract fails to ensure compliance with Maryland law regarding waivers of rights, potentially leading to disputes over the enforceability of the waiver. While not explicitly illegal to *include* a jury waiver, it must be knowingly and voluntarily made. Removing the explicit waiver introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 3-405
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 3-405
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law outlines the process for jury trials. While a jury waiver is not inherently illegal, its removal from this section makes the agreement ambiguous. The removal creates uncertainty if the parties truly agreed to waive their right to a jury trial, possibly contradicting principles ensuring knowing and voluntary relinquishment of rights under MD law, leading to potential legal challenges.

(a) (1) If declaratory relief is sought, a person who has or claims any interest which would be affected by the declaration, shall be made a party. (2) Except in a class action, the declaration may not prejudice the rights of any person not a party to the proceeding. (b) In any proceeding which involves the validity of a municipal or county ordinance or franchise, the municipality or county shall be made a party and is entitled to be heard. (c) If the statute, municipal or county ordinance, or franchise is alleged to be unconstitutional, the Attorney General need not be made a party but, immediately after suit has been filed, shall be served with a copy of the proceedings by certified mail. He is entitled to be heard, submit his views in writing within a time deemed reasonable by the court, or seek intervention pursuant to the Maryland Rules. perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly waived the right to a jury trial. The changed text removes this waiver, creating ambiguity about whether the parties knowingly and voluntarily agreed to waive this right. The Law Explanation indicates this could contradict principles ensuring knowing and voluntary relinquishment of rights under Maryland law. Snippet 2, §3–405, discusses parties affected by a declaration needing to be made a party, and that the declaration may not prejudice the rights of any person not a party to the proceeding, suggesting the importance of ensuring all affected parties' rights are considered. While snippet 2 doesn't directly address jury waivers, it highlights the general legal concern for protecting individual rights within legal proceedings, which the removal of the explicit waiver undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at the rate of twelve percent (12.0%) per annum compounded monthly from the due date until such payment is received.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments that are not received within thirty (30) days after their due date will bear interest at a rate determined by LKPL.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the interest rate to be 'determined by LKPL,' the contract becomes potentially usurious depending on the rate LKPL chooses. This removes a consumer protection by allowing LKPL to set an unreasonably high interest rate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Usury Law, Commercial Law, Title 12, Subtitle 1
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland law sets limits on interest rates. Allowing LKPL to unilaterally determine the interest rate removes the protection against usurious rates, directly contradicting Maryland's usury laws. The 'rate determined by LKPL' clause could enable the imposition of interest exceeding the legal limit, violating consumer rights and Maryland law.

(a) (1) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c), (d), (e), and (f) of this section, a lender may charge interest at an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 8 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if there is a written agreement signed by the borrower which sets forth the stated rate of interest charged by the lender. (2) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is a certificate of deposit held by the borrower, the lender may charge interest at a rate not to exceed 2 percent in excess of the rate of interest payable on the certificate of deposit. (3) If a loan made under paragraph (1) of this subsection is secured by the pledge of collateral which is other than a savings account or if such loan is unsecured, the lender may charge a rate of interest not in excess of 18 percent. However, on a loan made on or after July 1, 1982, a lender may charge an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 24 percent per year on the unpaid principal balance, provided that: (i) If the loan is a renewal or refinancing of a loan made prior to July 1, 1982, the lender complies with § 12–116 of this subtitle; (ii) If the loan includes a provision for a rate of interest which may be adjusted by the lender during the term of the loan, the lender complies with § 12–118 of this subtitle; (iii) Upon the borrower’s default, if the loan is secured by personal property, the lender complies with § 12–115 of this subtitle concerning repossession and redemption of the goods securing the loan; (iv) If the loan is for the purchase of consumer goods, the loan contract complies with § 12–117 of this subtitle; and (v) The loan does not include a balloon payment, unless payment in full is due on demand or in one year or less. (b) (1) A lender may charge interest at any effective rate of simple interest on the unpaid principal balance of a loan if: (i) There is a written agreement signed by the borrow
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a fixed interest rate with a rate 'determined by LKPL,' effectively removing the consumer protection against usurious rates. Maryland law, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 2, sets limits on interest rates ('a lender may charge interest at an effective rate of simple interest not in excess of 8 percent per year'). By allowing LKPL to set the rate without a defined cap, the contract now has the potential for interest exceeding the legal limit, directly contradicting the law's intent to protect borrowers from excessive interest charges. This omission of a defined interest cap undermines a legally mandated consumer protection, creating a legal contradiction.
